title: UML Class Diagram — Online Shopping System
examTip: Class Diagrams capture structural domain models. Keep fields organized clearly. For shopping domains, ensure there is an 'Item' class separating the 'ShoppingCart' from the 'Product' to support varying order quantities per product without polluting product definitions. Represent relationships and multiplicities accurately.
theory:
A UML Class Diagram for an Online Shopping System captures the essential domain classes and attributes required to build an e-commerce platform. It maps relationships between customer accounts, shopping baskets, catalog items, and payments.

Classes and Fields:
1. Customer: Contains customer data (customerId, name, address, email) and methods for signup (register) and authentication (login).
2. ShoppingCart: Stores the active shopping state (cartId, createdDate) and manages item lists (addItem, removeItem, checkout).
3. Item: Represents a specific product instance added to the cart, holding transaction properties like price and quantity. This class decouples shopping carts from products.
4. Product: Contains catalog details (productId, name, description, stock) and inventory status checks (isAvailable).
5. Order: Holds finalized checkout records (orderId, orderDate, status, totalAmount) and shipping details (processPayment, shipOrder).
6. Payment: Coordinates payment transactions (paymentId, amount, method) and runs gateway authorizations (validateCard, authorize).

Associations and Multiplicities: A Customer has one ShoppingCart. A ShoppingCart holds multiple Items. An Item references a specific Product. When a Customer checks out, the ShoppingCart generates an Order. The Order is paid by a Payment.
keyPoints: Class Diagrams define structural components, attributes, methods, and relationships of e-commerce systems
The Customer class represents the central user account with registration and profile attributes
The Item class acts as an association class connecting ShoppingCart and Product to store specific checkout quantities
ShoppingCart manages active checkouts, while Order stores persistent records of completed transactions
Payment encapsulates processing logic and interfaces with external payment gateways
useCases: Drafting database schemas for e-commerce web applications and microservices
Writing object-oriented domain classes and CRUD endpoints in back-end frameworks
Designing system features such as stock management, order tracking, and invoicing modules
mermaid_code:
flowchart TD
  class_Customer["Customer
- customerId: String
- name: String
- address: String
- email: String
--
+ register: boolean
+ login: boolean"]:::umlClass
  class_ShoppingCart["ShoppingCart
- cartId: String
- createdDate: Date
--
+ addItem: void
+ removeItem: void
+ checkout: void"]:::umlClass
  class_Item["Item
- itemId: String
- price: double
- quantity: int
--
+ updateQuantity: void"]:::umlClass
  class_Product["Product
- productId: String
- name: String
- description: String
- stock: int
--
+ isAvailable: boolean"]:::umlClass
  class_Order["Order
- orderId: String
- orderDate: Date
- status: String
- totalAmount: double
--
+ processPayment: void
+ shipOrder: void"]:::umlClass
  class_Payment["Payment
- paymentId: String
- amount: double
- method: String
--
+ validateCard: boolean
+ authorize: boolean"]:::umlClass

  %% Associations
  class_Customer -->|"has 1"| class_ShoppingCart
  class_ShoppingCart -->|"holds"| class_Item
  class_Item -->|"references"| class_Product
  class_ShoppingCart -->|"generates"| class_Order
  class_Order -->|"paid by"| class_Payment
